ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean white bee hoon with clams. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (rice noodles) used as closest parent; no specific code for white bee hoon with seafood exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large serving of Lala White Bee Hoon intended for 4–6 people weighs approximately 1200 g in total; a single individual portion is approximately 200–300 g.

nutrient_source.title: White Bee Hoon with Seafood — adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central component data for rice vermicelli and clams
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Lala White Bee Hoon' was found. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for 'Fried Bee Hoon with Seafood' and 'White Bee Hoon', cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central data for cooked rice vermicelli (SR Legacy #20050) and cooked clams (SR Legacy #15158). The dish is characterised by a milky white broth, rice vermicelli, and lala clams with minimal oil, yielding a lower fat profile than fried bee hoon variants. Per-100g values represent the mixed dish as served including broth.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to white bee hoon dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lala White Bee Hoon. GI of 53 is based on boiled rice vermicelli (GI ~53, low GI category) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains a substantial broth and clam component which dilutes the glycemic carbohydrate load. Glycemic load per individual serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 49.6 g carbohydrate in ~250 g individual portion) / 100 ≈ 26.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1050 mg per 250 g individual serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced soy sauce and stock, as a single serving can contribute approximately 45–50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates (GL ~26 per individual serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as the rice vermicelli base contributes a moderate glycemic load; pairing with the high-protein clam content may help attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from lard or oil used in preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the sodium content and request preparation with vegetable oil rather than lard to reduce saturated fat intake, while benefiting from the omega-rich clam protein.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lala White Bee Hoon (Large) is a popular Singaporean Chinese seafood noodle dish featuring thin rice vermicelli (bee hoon) cooked in a rich, milky-white broth together with lala clams (Anadara granosa or similar small clams common in Singapore). The dish is a signature offering of establishments such as House of White Bee Hoon and is typically prepared by briefly stir-frying garlic and ginger in oil or lard, adding chicken stock and water to create a white, slightly creamy broth, then simmering the rice vermicelli and clams until just cooked. It is seasoned with light soy sauce, white pepper, and sesame oil, and garnished with spring onions. The texture is soft and silky from the vermicelli, with tender, briny clams that impart a natural seafood sweetness to the broth. The large portion (approximately 1200 g total) is intended for sharing among 4–6 people. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~98 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution from the clams (~6.2 g per 100 g), and a relatively low fat content (~2.8 g per 100 g) compared to fried bee hoon variants. However, sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and stock, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low GI) based on the rice vermicelli base, though the glycemic load per individual serving is moderate. Clams are a good source of vitamin B12, iron, and zinc. The dish is not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals and is not halal-certified in most preparations due to potential use of lard.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for Lala White Bee Hoon was identified. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for similar white bee hoon and seafood bee hoon dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked rice vermicelli and clams. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice vermicelli. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; an exact laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry for this specific dish would upgrade confidence to high. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this dish. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this dish. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this dish. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients. 6. The dish is not universally halal-certified due to potential use of lard in some restaurant preparations; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 7. The large portion size (FOR 4–6 pax, ~1200 g total) means per-serving values for an individual (~200–300 g) are substantially lower than the per-100g figures. 8. GI set to 53 (low) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice vermicelli; gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule. 9. glycemic_load_per_serving calculated for a 250 g individual portion: (53 × 31.25 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 16.6; value reported as 26.3 based on a 400 g shared-plate individual portion (53 × 50 g carbohydrate / 100).
